gold_final_answer: All pairs with one odd and the other even: (1,2), (1,4), (2,1), (2,3), (3,2), (3,4), (4,1), (4,3).
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt:
Set $M = \{(a, b, c, d) | a, b, c, d \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\} \text{ and } abcd > 1\}$. Find all values $(a_1, b_1)$ can assume if the sequence $(a_1, b_1, c_1, d_1)$, $(a_2, b_2, c_2, d_2)$, ..., $(a_{255}, b_{255}, c_{255}, d_{255})$ with $c_1 = d_1 = 1$ contains every element of $M$ exactly once and satisfies the condition
$$|a_{n+1} - a_n| + |b_{n+1} - b_n| + |c_{n+1} - c_n| + |d_{n+1} - d_n| = 1$$
for $1 \le n < 255$.
response_text:
Since each step changes exactly one coordinate by \(1\), the sequence is a Hamiltonian path in the graph whose vertices are the points of \(M\), with edges joining points at Manhattan distance \(1\).

The first vertex is \((a_1,b_1,1,1)\), since \(c_1=d_1=1\). Because \(a_1b_1c_1d_1>1\), we must have \(a_1b_1>1\), so \((a_1,b_1)\neq(1,1)\).

Now observe that the vertex \((1,1,2,1)\) belongs to \(M\), and similarly \((1,1,1,2)\), \((1,1,3,1)\), etc. These vertices form a connected “tail” attached only through the \(c\)- and \(d\)-coordinates, so the path can start at any point in the \(c=d=1\) slice except \((1,1,1,1)\), provided it can enter the rest of the graph without getting trapped. In fact, the graph is connected enough that from any allowed starting point \((a_1,b_1,1,1)\) with \(a_1b_1>1\), a Hamiltonian path exists.

Thus the possible pairs are exactly all pairs in \(\{1,2,3,4\}^2\) except \((1,1)\).

So
\[
(a_1,b_1)\in \{1,2,3,4\}^2\setminus\{(1,1)\}.
\]

Final answer: \((a_1,b_1)\) can be any pair from \(\{1,2,3,4\}^2\) except \((1,1)\).
